content:
6 min read
Just now

--

This post uses code-review-graph by tirth8205 as a running example. All credit for the tool, benchmarks, and architecture described below goes to the original project. Go star it and read the source if this post makes you curious.

1. The problem GraphRAG solves
Ask an AI coding assistant to review a pull request, and here’s what often happens behind the scenes: it doesn’t just read the diff. It reads the file the diff lives in. Then, to understand whether the change is safe, it starts reading the files that import it. Then maybe the tests. Then a few more files it thinks might be related, just to be safe.
Multiply that across a large repo and you get a familiar pattern: slow reviews, expensive token bills, and, counterintuitively, worse answers, because the model’s attention gets diluted across a pile of code that has nothing to do with the actual change.
This is just the classic RAG problem (retrieval quality determines answer quality) showing up in a new place. Most people encounter RAG in the context of chatbots answering questions from documents. But the same principle applies to AI coding tools. What you retrieve and hand to the model matters as much as how good the model is. Feed it the wrong context, and even a great model will give you a mediocre answer.

Diagram from code-review-graph, used with credit to the original repo.
2. What is GraphRAG, conceptually?
There are two broad strategies for deciding what to retrieve.
Naive RAG chunks text, embeds each chunk, and does similarity search. It pulls the top-k chunks that look most alike to the query and stuffs them into the context window. This works reasonably well for prose, where meaning tends to live in the words themselves.
It works much worse for code. A function’s importance in a codebase usually has nothing to do with how similar its variable names are to your query. It has to do with who calls it, what it depends on, and what breaks if it changes. That’s relational information, and embedding similarity doesn’t capture relationships.
GraphRAG takes a different approach. Instead of retrieving by similarity, you first build a structural map of the codebase (nodes for functions, classes, and files, edges for calls, inheritance, imports, and test coverage), and then retrieve by traversing that graph.
code-review-graph is a clean illustration of this pipeline in practice:

• Tree-sitter parses source files into an AST for each of 24+ supported languages

• The AST is converted into a graph, where nodes are functions, classes, and files, and edges capture calls, inheritance, imports, and test relationships

• At review time, the graph is queried, not re-read from scratch. The AI assistant asks structural questions (“what calls this function?”, “what does this change affect?”) instead of scanning files
That shift, from search over text to traversal over structure, is the essence of GraphRAG.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Diagram from code-review-graph, used with credit to the original repo.
3. Four GraphRAG concepts, each grounded in a real feature
Rather than explain GraphRAG in the abstract, here are four core concepts, each mapped to something code-review-graph actually implements.
Blast radius → graph traversal
When a file changes, the tool doesn’t ask “what looks similar to this file?” It asks “what’s reachable from this node?” It runs a BFS/DFS traversal outward from the changed function to find every caller, dependent, and test that could be affected. The result is called the change’s “blast radius.”
What this teaches: graphs let you answer “what’s connected to this?” This is a question flat text search fundamentally cannot answer, because text search has no concept of connection, only similarity.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Diagram from code-review-graph, used with credit to the original repo.
Hub and bridge detection → betweenness centrality
Some functions in a codebase are quietly load-bearing, not because they’re big or frequently changed, but because they sit on the path between many other parts of the system. Graph theory has a name for this: betweenness centrality. Nodes with high betweenness are “bridges,” chokepoints where a lot of traffic (in this case, call paths) passes through.
What this teaches: graph metrics can reveal structural importance that has nothing to do with a file’s content, length, or recent activity. It’s the kind of importance a text-based search would never surface.
Community detection → the Leiden algorithm
Codebases have implicit architecture (modules, layers, subsystems) that often isn’t documented anywhere. Community detection algorithms (here, Leiden) cluster densely-connected nodes together automatically, effectively reverse-engineering the module boundaries from how the code actually calls itself.
What this teaches: a graph built from real relationships can surface architecture nobody wrote down, simply by looking at connection density.
Edge confidence tiers → EXTRACTED / INFERRED / AMBIGUOUS
Not every edge in a code graph is equally certain. A direct function call is unambiguous. A call resolved through dynamic dispatch, a decorator, or a loosely-typed language might be inferred with lower confidence. code-review-graph tags each edge with a confidence tier and a float score rather than treating all relationships as equally solid.
What this teaches: building a graph from real-world code isn’t a clean, deterministic process. It’s probabilistic, and a good system represents that uncertainty explicitly instead of hiding it.
4. Why this matters, the evidence, not just the theory
None of this is worth much if it doesn’t actually reduce token usage or improve results. code-review-graph publishes benchmarks across several real open-source repositories, and the headline numbers are worth citing as evidence, not just theory. On average, the graph-based approach used a fraction of the tokens a naive full-file read would require, with the gap widening dramatically on large monorepos where most of the codebase is irrelevant to any single change.
It’s worth being honest about the trade-off, too. The project’s own benchmarks show its impact analysis catches every affected file (perfect recall) but sometimes flags extra files that turn out not to matter (lower precision). That’s a deliberate, sensible choice. For code review, missing an affected file is a much worse failure than over-including one. It’s a good reminder that engineering is rarely about a free lunch. It’s about picking which trade-off you’re comfortable with.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Diagram from code-review-graph, used with credit to the original repo.
5. The takeaway
Zoom out past this one tool, and the lesson generalizes. Plain embeddings capture semantic similarity, things that sound alike. Graphs capture relationships, things that are actually connected. For any domain with strong relational structure (codebases, org charts, citation networks, legal precedent, supply chains), graph-based retrieval tends to beat naive similarity search, because the question you actually want answered (“what does this affect?”) isn’t a similarity question at all. It’s a connectivity question.
That’s the real shift GraphRAG represents. Instead of handing an AI a pile of text and hoping the right pages float to the top, you hand it a map and let it navigate.

Full credit to tirth8205 for building and open-sourcing code-review-graph, MIT licensed. If this post got you curious about how it works under the hood, the repo is the best place to go next.
